chapters.title: Introduction
chapters.keyIdea: Nice Guys are men conditioned over five decades of social change to constantly seek the approval of others — this book is the proven plan for breaking free and finally getting what you want in love, sex, and life.

content.text: Over the last five decades, the world has seen more social change than in any comparable period of history. Along with these social changes has come a new kind of man — a man who believes that if he is good, giving, and caring, he will in return be happy, loved, and fulfilled. Dr. Glover calls this man the Nice Guy.

content.label: The Nice Guy's Core Belief
content.text: Nice Guys believe: if I am good enough, giving enough, and caring enough, then people will love me, meet my needs, and life will work smoothly. This belief is the central illusion of the Nice Guy Syndrome. It does not work — because it is a covert contract with life that life never signed.

Dr. Glover spent years as a Nice Guy himself. He struggled to hide his own mistakes and imperfections. He caretook the people around him. He gave in order to get. He kept the peace at all costs. He avoided conflict. He sought the approval of everyone he met. He tried to fix the problems of those around him. He wanted everyone to be happy, but especially wanted everyone to be happy with him.

Nice Guys are actually not very nice at all. They are dishonest. They are secretive. They are manipulative. They are controlling. They are resentful. They are passive-aggressive. And they are often full of quiet, inexplicable rage — the direct result of a lifetime of giving to get and watching the covert contract go unmet.

This book is the result of Dr. Glover's own recovery and the work he has done with hundreds of Nice Guys through individual and group therapy. The No More Mr. Nice Guy! men's recovery group became the testing ground for the ideas in this book. The men who worked through this program experienced dramatic changes in every area of their lives.

content.label: What This Book Will Help You Do
content.text: Effective ways to get needs met. Feel powerful and confident. Create intimate, passionate relationships. Express feelings. Have a satisfying sex life. Embrace your masculinity. Live up to your full potential. Accept yourself just as you are. Make your wants and needs a priority. Stop seeking the approval of others and start getting what you really want in love, sex, and life.

Ask yourself honestly: Do you seek others' approval before trusting your own judgment? Do you hide your flaws and mistakes? Do you do things for others hoping they will reciprocate? Do you feel resentful that your efforts go unnoticed? If yes — you are a Nice Guy. This book is your roadmap out. The journey begins now.

chapters.title: The Nice Guy Syndrome
chapters.keyIdea: Nice Guys are everywhere — beneath their giving, helpful surface runs a covert contract that breeds dishonesty, manipulation, and dysfunction — and the cure is not becoming a jerk but becoming an integrated man who is direct, honest, and unapologetically himself.

content.text: Jason is a chiropractor in his mid-thirties. He opens a session: 'I'm a Nice Guy.' His sex life has been dead for months. That morning, he spent an hour getting the baby ready so his wife could sleep in — she criticizes his choice of outfit for the baby. He cleans the kitchen before she wakes up — she notices he hasn't wiped the counters. 'All I want is to be loved and appreciated,' he says, clearly baffled by how little return he gets on such enormous investment.

Omar carries a pager so his friends can always reach him. Todd has many female friends but can't get a date. Bill can't say no — he fixes cars at church and coaches little league while his own life falls apart. Gary's wife rages at him, and he is always the first to apologize. Rick is gay and living with an alcoholic partner. Lyle is a devout Christian deacon with a secret pornography addiction. Jose has been with a needy, dependent woman for five years and has been thinking of leaving since the day she moved in. All of these men are Nice Guys.

content.label: The Nice Guy's Core Script
content.text: Every Nice Guy, regardless of background or personality, operates from the same core belief: 'If I am good and do everything right, I will be loved, get my needs met, and have a problem-free life.' This belief is their life paradigm — the road map they follow. The tragedy is that this road map will never take them where they want to go.

The Nice Guy is everywhere. He's the husband who lets his wife run the show at home while he silently seethes. He's the buddy who will do anything for his friends yet whose own life is in shambles. He's the boss who tells people what they want to hear instead of what they need to hear. He's the man whose life seems perfectly under control — until suddenly, without warning, everything falls apart.

content.label: Characteristics of the Nice Guy
content.text: Nice Guys are givers. They fix and caretake. They seek approval. They avoid conflict. They hide their perceived flaws and mistakes. They seek 'the right way' to do everything. They repress their feelings. They try to be different from their fathers. They are more comfortable with women than men. They have difficulty making their own needs a priority. They make their partner their emotional center of gravity. They attract needy or unavailable partners.

content.label: The Not-So-Nice Traits Beneath the Surface
content.text: Nice Guys are fundamentally dishonest. They are secretive. They compartmentalize their lives. They are manipulative. They are controlling. They give to get. They are passive-aggressive. They are full of repressed rage. They have addictive tendencies. They have difficulty setting boundaries. They are frequently isolated. They are attracted to people and situations that need fixing. They have problems in intimate relationships. They have issues with sexuality. They underperform in their careers.

Women often describe the Nice Guy as a Dr. Jekyll and Mr. Hyde character. On the surface he seems loving and giving — but then something happens and the rage, resentment, or passive-aggression appears. Women in Dr. Glover's groups have said: 'He seems so nice, but nothing I do ever seems to make him happy,' and 'He says he doesn't care, but then he pouts for days.' The women who live with Nice Guys often sense something is fundamentally off — they just can't name what it is.

When Gil first joined a No More Mr. Nice Guy men's group he was afraid it would turn him into a selfish, insensitive jerk. Recovery from the Nice Guy Syndrome is not about becoming a bad boy or a jerk. It is about becoming an integrated man — a man who has a clear sense of himself, takes responsibility for his life, sets boundaries, and gives because he wants to, not because he fears what will happen if he doesn't.

content.label: The Integrated Man
content.text: An integrated man: has a good sense of himself; is comfortable in his own skin; sets limits and is comfortable with boundaries; is direct and straightforward with men and women; does not seek the approval of others; accepts himself just as he is; is able to ask for what he wants; takes responsibility for his own needs; embraces his masculinity; lives with integrity; is not ashamed of his sexual nature.

Jason's Nice Guy paradigm was forged by two parents. His father was controlling and perfectionistic — directing every detail of the family's life, including Jason's adult career, his house, his car, his church. His mother had no independent social life and turned to her children for emotional fulfillment. Jason learned: hide mistakes, attend to neediness, never be a problem. Now his wife is emotionally similar to both parents. When his Nice Guy paradigm fails to produce the love and approval he expects, his only option — according to the paradigm — is to try harder and do more of the same.

content.label: The Ineffective Nice Guy Paradigm
content.text: If I can hide my flaws and become what I think others want me to be, then I will be loved, get my needs met, and have a problem-free life. This paradigm is developed when young and naive, is mostly unconscious, feels like reality, and is assumed to be 100% accurate. Paradigms work like road maps — if the map is wrong, trying harder won't get you where you're going. It will just get you more lost, faster.

In a famous Seinfeld episode, George Costanza's life has reached an all-time low. A friend tells him that every decision he has ever made has been wrong — and that if he wants his life to change, he has to do the opposite of every instinct he has. George takes the advice. He approaches an attractive woman with painful honesty about his failures. She finds him fascinating. The rest of the episode shows that doing the opposite of everything he had always done transforms his life. Nice Guys need to understand this lesson: if what you're doing isn't working, you need to do something different.

content.label: What Recovery Looks Like
content.text: Recovering Nice Guys: accept themselves just as they are; stop seeking approval; experience loving and satisfying relationships; make their needs a priority; find people who help them meet their needs; give with no strings attached; face their fears; develop their own integrity; build supportive male relationships; have a fulfilling sex life; find their passion and purpose; become truly peaceful and contented.

This week, identify three situations where you acted like a Nice Guy — where you did something primarily to gain approval, avoid disapproval, or keep the peace. Write them down. Don't try to change yet — just observe with honesty. Then identify one man who seems integrated — who is both direct and kind, both boundaried and caring. Ask yourself what one thing you could learn from how he moves through the world.

chapters.title: The Making Of A Nice Guy
chapters.keyIdea: Nice Guys are not born — they are made by childhood experiences of abandonment that created toxic shame, and then shaped further by Baby Boom social forces that told boys that being male was problematic.

content.text: The Nice Guy Syndrome develops because it does not feel safe or acceptable for a boy to be just who he is. The process starts early. During the first five years of life, children are totally dependent on their caregivers — abandonment literally means death. And children are egocentric — they believe they cause everything. When a caregiver is absent, raging, depressed, critical, or simply unavailable, the child draws one inevitable conclusion: there must be something wrong with me.

content.label: Abandonment Experiences That Create Nice Guys
content.text: A parent who is physically absent. A parent who is emotionally unavailable. A parent who is depressed, addicted, or mentally ill. A parent who rages or is physically abusive. A parent who is sexually abusive. A parent who gives love conditionally. A parent who is smothering or overprotective. A family environment of constant tension or conflict. A family with rigid religious rules. Any experience that creates the toxic message: 'I am not OK just as I am.'

content.label: Toxic Shame: The Engine of the Nice Guy
content.text: Toxic shame is not 'I did something bad' — it is 'I AM bad.' It is the belief that one is fundamentally defective, different, or unlovable. It is not based on actual wrongdoing. It is a distorted conclusion drawn by a helpless child who could not get his needs met and decided it must be because something was fundamentally wrong with him. Every self-defeating behavior of the Nice Guy flows directly from this toxic shame.

Alan is the oldest of three children. His father was an abusive alcoholic who abandoned the family when Alan was seven. Alan made a decision then: he would be 180 degrees different from his father. His mother raised him in a fundamentalist Christian church that taught him his normal impulses made him a sinner. She regularly commiserated with Alan about his father's badness — explicitly wanting to raise her sons differently. Today, as an adult, Alan is still emotionally close to his mother. He seeks her approval on major life decisions. He has never fully separated.

Jason thought he had a 'Leave It to Beaver' family. His father was the picture of success — he closely directed every aspect of family life, managed Jason's adult career, told him what house to buy and what church to attend. His mother had no friends of her own and turned to her children for affirmation and emotional support. Jason felt chronically inadequate next to his seemingly perfect parents. His survival strategy: hide mistakes, attend to neediness, never be a problem. Thirty years later, he has married a woman emotionally similar to both of them.

Jose was the second of seven children. He took on a parenting role at age 14 when his father became abusive and controlling. His mother was manic-depressive — grandiose during manic phases, suicidal during depressive ones. At 15, Jose had to break down a door and take a gun from his suicidal mother's hands. He came to see his purpose as fixing chaos everywhere. As an adult he is consistently drawn to dependent women and survivors of sexual abuse — people who need rescuing. His caretaking is unconscious, compulsive, and completely ineffective at getting his own needs met.

content.label: Two Kinds of Nice Guys
content.text: The 'I'm So Bad' Nice Guy: exaggerates his shame, lists his bad behaviors to anyone who will listen, never quite believes anyone really buys his Nice Guy persona. The 'I'm So Good' Nice Guy: represses shame in an airtight compartment, is genuinely convinced he is the nicest, most giving person you will meet, masks shame with good deeds. Both operate from the same core paradigm. The difference is only in the direction they turn the shame — inward or outward.

Toxic shame produces survival mechanisms: coping strategies designed to prevent re-experiencing the original abandonment pain. These mechanisms — becoming needless, people-pleasing, hiding flaws, caretaking — create the illusion of safety. The cruel irony is that they are the very behaviors that prevent Nice Guys from getting what they most desperately want: genuine love and authentic connection.

The Baby Boom generation faced unique social dynamics that magnified Nice Guy conditioning. In 1910, one-third of American families lived on farms — fathers and sons worked side by side. By 1940, only one in five. By 1970, ninety-six percent of Americans lived in urban environments. Industrial capitalism moved fathers out of the home and into offices and factories. The number of female-headed households rose from five million in 1950 to thirteen million by 1970. Most Nice Guys from the Baby Boom era were effectively raised by their mothers alone — women who inherited the job of turning boys into men.

content.label: Three Social Forces That Made Baby Boom Nice Guys
content.text: (1) Separated from fathers and male role models — boys lost the daily experience of masculinity in action and were left to construct an identity largely from feminine models. (2) Raised primarily by women — boys received an essentially female blueprint for relating: be sensitive, don't fight, always be nice, put others first. (3) Radical feminism implied men were problematic — epithets like 'all men are pigs,' the slogan 'a woman needs a man like a fish needs a bicycle,' and the broader cultural message that traditional masculinity was toxic gave boys who were already looking to women for definition one more reason to be ashamed of who they were.

The Vietnam War crystallized the alienation between many Baby Boom boys and their fathers. A generation of WWII veterans could not understand their sons' social rebellion and draft resistance. Battle lines were drawn between young men protesting a war started and perpetuated by their fathers. The antiwar movement created a new breed of American male focused on love, peace, and avoiding conflict. Meanwhile, many mothers were working to raise their sons differently from their husbands — peaceful, giving, nurturing, and attentive to women's needs.

Robert Bly, the author of Iron John, called the product of these social forces the 'soft male' — a new breed of American man who is lovely and gentle but not happy, visibly lacking energy, life-preserving but not life-giving, often seen with strong women who radiate the life energy the man himself has little to offer. Camille Paglia observed that the 'hard-driving woman' was solving her own dilemma by finding a 'nice, malleable boy-man who becomes another son in the subliminally matriarchal household.'

Dr. Glover observes that Nice Guy conditioning did not end with the Baby Boom. He sees young men in their twenties and teens demonstrating all the characteristics of the Nice Guy Syndrome — not only shaped by the same social dynamics, but now growing up in single-parent households or with Nice Guy fathers. As he wrote the first edition of this book, he expected they were entering the third generation of Nice Guys.

content.label: The Habits of Highly Ineffective Men
content.text: Nice Guys prevent themselves from getting what they want in love and life by: seeking the approval of others; trying to hide their perceived flaws and mistakes; putting other people's needs and wants before their own; sacrificing their personal power and playing the role of a victim; disassociating themselves from other men and their own masculine energy; co-creating relationships that are less than satisfying; creating situations in which they do not have very much good sex; failing to live up to their full potential.

Write your own story of how you became a Nice Guy. What were your abandonment experiences? Identify the key messages you received — from your father's absence or presence, from your mother's needs, from school, church, or culture — that taught you it was not OK to be just who you are. Fill in these blanks honestly: 'There must be something wrong with me because ___.' And: 'I'm only good enough and lovable when ___.' Share what you write with one safe person.

chapters.title: Learn To Please Yourself
chapters.keyIdea: Nice Guys have built their entire lives around seeking external approval — becoming a chameleon who hides everything real — and recovery requires the radical act of approving of yourself first, through self-care, self-disclosure, and time alone.

content.text: 'I'm a chameleon,' reveals Todd, a 30-year-old single Nice Guy. 'I will become whatever I believe a person wants me to be in order to be liked. With my smart friends I act intelligent. Around my mother, I look like the perfect loving son. With the guys at work I cuss and swear. I'm not sure who I really am or if any of them would like me just for who I am. If I can't figure out what people want me to be, I'm afraid I'll be all alone. The funny thing is, I feel alone most of the time anyway.'

Just about everything a Nice Guy does is consciously or unconsciously calculated to gain someone's approval or to avoid disapproval. Nice Guys seek this external validation in just about every relationship and social situation — even from strangers and people they don't even like. By trying to please everyone, Nice Guys often end up pleasing no one — including themselves.

content.label: The Approval Trap
content.text: Because Nice Guys do not believe they are OK just as they are, they use attachments to try to convince themselves and others that they are valuable. These attachments can be something about themselves (appearance, talent, intellect), something they do (act nice, work hard, dance well), or something external (attractive wife, cute child, nice car). Without these attachments, Nice Guys don't know what else about themselves would make anyone love them. Being a Nice Guy — being 'good' and doing it 'right' — is the ultimate attachment.

Cal is a good illustration. Cal tries to get external validation by always being in a good mood, driving a nice car, dressing well, having a cute daughter, and having an attractive wife. He likes to dress his 14-month-old daughter in a cute dress and take her to the park, knowing people will smile and comment. From the moment he begins dressing her, he is unconsciously attaching his value and identity to the acknowledgment he thinks he will receive for being a 'good dad.' The irony: no one really values Cal for his attachments. His dependency on external validation actually prevents people from getting to know him as a person.

Nice Guys seek external validation everywhere, but their quest for approval is most pronounced with women. Nice Guys interpret a woman's approval as the ultimate validation of their worth. Signs of approval — a smile, desire for sex, flirtatious behavior, attentiveness — send a Nice Guy into a state of euphoria. Signs of disapproval — a bad mood, silence, irritability — trigger immediate anxiety. This dynamic has three profound consequences.

content.label: Three Costs of Seeking Women's Approval
content.text: (1) It requires Nice Guys to constantly monitor a woman's emotional state — her mood becomes the barometer of their entire world. Dr. Glover calls this the 'possibility of availability' — Nice Guys are hyper-vigilant even toward women they have no sexual interest in. (2) It gives women the power to set the tone of the relationship — if she is happy, so is he; if she is angry or depressed, he will feel anxious until she feels better. (3) It creates rage toward women. Though most Nice Guys claim to love women, they actually carry tremendous resentment — because we eventually despise whatever we make into our god, especially when it fails to deliver.

Since Nice Guys do not believe they are OK just as they are, they see any mistake or perceived flaw as proof that they are bad and unlovable. As a result, Nice Guys are consummate cover-up artists. They believe they must hide or distract attention from any shortcoming — if they forget something, if they are late, if they break something, if they don't understand something, if they do something wrong, if they are depressed, if they are in pain. Son Steve poked holes in the kitchen table and immediately told his mother. Dr. Glover reflects: if he had done that as a child, he would have had an attack of toxic shame and tried his best to hide it.

content.label: How Nice Guys Hide the Evidence
content.text: Lying: Nice Guys pride themselves on honesty, but they will tell lies, partial truths, and omit information if it prevents negative attention. Drawing on their account: since they strive to be so good and giving, they believe their good deeds should wipe out any mistakes — if he does most things right, no one should notice the few things he does wrong. Fixing: rather than apologizing and taking responsibility, Nice Guys try to fix situations by doing whatever gets the other person to stop being upset. DEER Response: Defend, Explain, Excuse, Rationalize — fear-based behaviors used to distract from mistakes. Shame dumping: when confronted, redirect attention to the other person's flaws. Walls: addictions, humor, sarcasm, intellectualism, perfectionism, isolation — whatever keeps people at a distance.

Dr. Glover calls Nice Guys Teflon Men. They work so hard to be smooth that nothing can stick to them. Like a perfectly smooth surface, their cover-up strategy is also what makes it impossible for people to get close. Humans connect through shared imperfection, shared problems, shared humanity — not through performed perfection. It is a person's rough edges that give others something to connect with. The more perfectly the Nice Guy hides himself, the more alone he becomes.

Recovery from the Nice Guy Syndrome involves changing core paradigms. Rather than seeking external validation and avoiding disapproval, recovering Nice Guys must begin seeking their own approval. Ironically, when Nice Guys begin focusing on pleasing themselves, they actually begin to experience the intimacy and connection with others that they have always desperately craved. Self-approval has five pathways: identifying approval-seeking behavior, taking good care of the self, giving positive affirmations, spending extended periods alone, and revealing the self to safe people.

Cal decided to stop washing his car for one month — one of his primary approval-seeking attachments. Since he lives in the Seattle area, his car soon developed a gray haze of rain and road grime. When he drove to work or a friend's house, he was sure people were looking at his filthy car and judging him. When his daughter drew pictures in the dirt with her finger, it was almost more than he could stand. At the end of the month, Cal washed and waxed his car. Surprisingly, not one person had commented on the dirty car, and no one had quit liking him or removed their love from him.

Todd, introduced at the opening of the chapter, spent so much time trying to get the approval of others that he rarely did anything for himself. On the encouragement of his No More Mr. Nice Guy group, he began small — buying himself new socks and underwear. After a few weeks he started an exercise program. Even though it made him feel guilty, he started going to a massage therapist every other week. After six months, he decided to spend $2,000 to join a singles activity club. Even though he occasionally heard a little voice that said he wasn't worth it, doing so was one of the most affirming things he had ever done. A couple of months later, he reported going on two dates with two different women, both of whom seemed to like him just as he was.

Reid, a recovering alcoholic in a No More Mr. Nice Guy group, came into group one evening and stayed quiet for the first forty minutes. When Dr. Glover asked how he was doing, Reid whispered, 'I almost didn't come tonight. I've done something so terrible I don't know if I can tell you.' As the group offered support, Reid broke through his shame and revealed he had relapsed on vodka, gone to peep shows twice, and felt totally worthless and like he didn't want to go on living. Tears ran down his face. The group didn't judge him. They supported him, called the behavior out, and made plans with him. When Reid left that night he had released a heavy burden and received the message that there was nothing he could do that would make these men stop liking him or stop caring about him.

content.label: Shedding Old Skin
content.text: As recovering Nice Guys release their toxic shame and start seeking their own approval, they begin to realize: they are not bad; they don't have to do anything to win other people's approval; they don't have to hide their perceived flaws or mistakes; people can love them just as they are. By shedding their chameleon skin and learning to please themselves, recovering Nice Guys begin to experience the intimacy and connection they have always desired — and radiate a life energy and charisma that draws people to them.

Do one thing each day purely for yourself — not to impress anyone, not to earn approval. It can be as simple as eating food you actually want, taking a walk with no destination, spending an hour on something you enjoy. Then identify your primary approval-seeking attachment — the thing you rely on most to get people to like you. Put a moratorium on it for one month. Observe what happens to your anxiety and what happens to your relationships.

chapters.title: Make Your Needs A Priority
chapters.keyIdea: Nice Guys appear needless and wantless while secretly drowning in unmet needs — they use covert contracts and caretaking to get their needs met indirectly, triggering the Victim Triangle, and recovery requires the radical act of putting themselves first.

content.text: 'I want you to know that I'm really uncomfortable with that thing we talked about last week.' Lars, an anxiety-filled executive, began his second counseling session this way. In the first session, Dr. Glover had asked Lars what he did for himself. Lars gave a puzzled look: 'What do you mean?' After a long pause: 'Not much, I guess.' The discussion about making his needs a priority was met with both fear and resistance. 'That would make me selfish and self-centered,' Lars said. 'That would make me too much like my old man.'

Lars is a typical Nice Guy when it comes to his needs. Nice Guys generally focus their attention on meeting everyone else's needs while trying to be 'low maintenance' kinds of guys themselves. This pattern is the direct result of childhood conditioning: when a child's needs are not met in a timely, healthy manner, he may come to believe he is 'bad' for having needs, or that it is his needs that cause people to hurt or abandon him. Rather than facing this pain, Nice Guys develop a set of survival mechanisms.

content.label: Four Survival Mechanisms That Prevent Needs Being Met
content.text: (1) Trying to appear needless and wantless — since having needs seemed to drive people away in childhood, Nice Guys try to eliminate or hide all needs, believing survival depends on appearing not to have any. (2) Making it difficult for others to give to them — Nice Guys are terrible receivers; they are terrified of getting what they really want and will unconsciously ensure they don't get it. (3) Using covert contracts — giving in order to get, with unsaid agreements the other person never agreed to. (4) Caretaking — focusing on others' problems and needs to avoid dealing with their own, while expecting a return they never name.

Beneath the facade of needlessness, all Nice Guys are actually extremely needy. Consequently, when they try to get their needs met, Nice Guys are frequently indirect, unclear, manipulative, and controlling. They also unconsciously ensure they won't get what they want — by connecting with needy or unavailable partners, starting fights at intimate moments, being unclear about what they want, or focusing so completely on their partner's pleasure that they remain invisible themselves.

content.label: The Covert Contract
content.text: Almost everything a Nice Guy does is based on a covert contract: I will do X for you, so that you will do X for me. We will both act as if we have no awareness of this contract. Most of us have had the experience of whispering 'I love you' to a partner and waiting expectantly for 'I love you too' in return. This is the simplest form of covert contract — giving to get. Since the other person never agreed to the contract and usually doesn't even know it exists, they almost never deliver — and the Nice Guy feels cheated, victimized, and resentful.

Reese, a graphic designer in his late twenties, lamented: 'Why can't I find a partner who gives as much back to me as I give to him?' Within a year, he entered three intense relationships. Each failed for the same reason: Reese picked men who needed rescuing or fixing. The first had just gotten off drugs and never applied for a work visa — Reese later learned he was HIV positive, something he had failed to mention. The second could never commit because of conflict between his homosexuality and his religion. The third was in the military with no car — Reese quit his job, sold his car, and moved to be with him, only to return three months later when his partner started cheating. During this twelve-month period, Reese gave up his job and alienated most of his friends and family. No matter how much he gave, he never felt he got as much back.

content.label: Caretaking vs. Caring
content.text: Caretaking: gives to others what the giver needs to give; comes from a place of emptiness within the giver; always has unconscious strings attached. Caring: gives to others what the receiver actually needs; comes from a place of abundance within the giver; has no strings attached. Nice Guys believe their caretaking is fundamentally loving and makes them good people. In reality, caretaking has nothing to do with being loving or good. Caretaking is an immature and indirect attempt to get one's own needs met.

Rather than helping Nice Guys get their needs met, covert contracts and caretaking only produce frustration and resentment. When this frustration builds long enough, it spills out in what Dr. Glover's wife calls 'victim pukes': rage attacks, passive-aggressive behavior, pouting, tantrums, withdrawing, shaming, criticizing, blaming. This is the Victim Triangle: (1) the Nice Guy gives to others hoping to get something in return; (2) when he doesn't get as much as he gives, he feels frustrated and resentful; (3) when the frustration builds, it spills out as victim behavior — after which the cycle begins all over again.

Shane had his girlfriend Racquel on a pedestal, but deep inside he believed she could only love him if he was 'good enough.' To win her love he gave gifts, sent cards, left phone messages, bought her clothes, planned surprises, and helped with her home and children. This created a sense of emotional indebtedness for Racquel — she felt like she could never repay him. In time, the only way she could cope was by pushing him away. Shane was devastated. The more he gave, the more resentful he became. They would have explosive fights, break up, and then he would pursue her again, caretaking and pleasing to win her back. The cycle repeated itself endlessly.

Recovery from the Nice Guy Syndrome must center on learning to put themselves first and making their needs a priority. Since Nice Guys learned to sacrifice themselves in order to survive, this is the most terrifying shift imaginable. But it is also the most powerful: when Nice Guys stop operating from the assumption that they must earn love through self-sacrifice, they discover something unexpected — people are actually drawn to them more, not less.

Dr. Glover challenged each man in one of his groups to experiment with putting himself first for at least a week. Lars decided to make time every day to work out — something he had given up years before. His wife applied a little guilt: 'That's not fair that you get to work out and I don't.' Lars was tempted to back down and find a solution for her. Instead, he reflected her concern and told her he was going to work out anyway. After the third day, his wife started asking how his workout went. After the first week, she told him he had inspired her to start taking better care of herself.

Shane's plan for putting himself first involved paying attention to when he had an impulse to do something for Racquel — and then doing something for himself instead. When he felt the urge to buy her a gift, he bought himself something. When he felt the urge to call just to check on her, he called a group member instead. At the end of the week, Racquel reported she felt a lot less smothered by Shane and actually looked forward to spending time with him. She called one evening and invited him over to make love. Later, after six months of Shane focusing on himself and not giving gifts or surprises, both Shane and Racquel reported their relationship had never been better.

content.label: Benefits of Putting Yourself First
content.text: Nice Guys who put themselves first discover: they increase the likelihood of getting what they need and want; they can give judiciously — giving what people actually need, not what the giver needs to give; they can give without resentment and expectation; they become less needy; they become more attractive. Confidence and self-assurance are genuinely attractive. Helpless, whiny, and needy are not. Putting the self first doesn't drive people away — it draws them in.

Make a decision to put yourself first for one full week. Tell the people around you what you are doing. Identify three needs you have been meeting covertly through caretaking or covert contracts — and this week, ask for them directly. Notice the anxiety this creates. Notice what happens in your relationships when you stop operating from unconscious strings. At the end of the week, ask the people around you what it was like for them when you put yourself first. Their answer will likely surprise you.

chapters.title: Reclaim Your Personal Power
chapters.keyIdea: Nice Guys are wimpy victims of their own childhood survival mechanisms — reclaiming personal power means surrendering control, dwelling in reality, expressing feelings, facing fears, living with integrity, and setting boundaries.

content.text: Dr. Glover once told a men's group that Nice Guys are wimps. The room went dead silent. Then one man said, 'That's the meanest, most accurate thing anyone has ever said to me.' Nice Guys expend enormous energy trying to control how the world responds to them — managing perceptions, avoiding conflict, seeking approval — and when none of it works they experience themselves as helpless victims of an uncontrollable world. This is the Paradigm of Powerlessness, and it is the core of why Nice Guys feel stuck.

content.label: The Paradigm of Powerlessness
content.text: Nice Guys believe their happiness and wellbeing depend on forces outside themselves: their partner's mood, their boss's approval, the economy, their childhood, other people's behavior. Because they locate the source of their pain outside themselves, they also locate the solution there — and feel perpetually powerless. The shift to personal power requires a radical reframe: I am responsible for my own experience. Nothing outside me has the power to make or break me.

content.label: 13 Strategies Nice Guys Use to Keep Life Smooth
content.text: Telling people what they want to hear. Hiding mistakes and flaws. Suppressing feelings. Avoiding conflict. Seeking approval before acting. Doing things they don't want to do. Doing things for others hoping for reciprocation. Not asking directly for needs. Using sex or helpfulness to control others' emotional states. Staying in bad situations hoping things will change. Pretending to agree when they don't. Making themselves invisible. Putting others' needs first in every situation. All of these strategies have one thing in common: they are attempts to control the uncontrollable.

content.label: 6 Tools for Reclaiming Personal Power
content.text: (1) Surrender control — stop trying to manage outcomes and people's perceptions. Let go and trust yourself to handle whatever comes. (2) Dwell in reality — see your life clearly without the distortion of wishful thinking. (3) Express feelings — share your inner world with safe people and in appropriate situations. (4) Face your fears — fear is the primary driver of Nice Guy behavior. Do the thing that frightens you. (5) Live with integrity — let your actions reflect your values, even when it is uncomfortable. (6) Set boundaries — decide what is acceptable and unacceptable and act accordingly.

Gil had been in a relationship with a woman he knew was wrong for him for two years. He kept telling himself she would change, that he just needed to be more patient and understanding. In therapy he worked on the concept of surrender — accepting his girlfriend exactly as she was, with no expectation of change. The week after he genuinely surrendered, he broke up with her. He reported feeling more powerful than he had in years. Surrender is not the same as giving up. It is releasing the illusion of control and allowing reality to reveal what action is actually needed.

Les had been making excuses for his mediocre career and troubled marriage for years. He blamed his childhood, his wife's critical personality, and his lack of formal education. In therapy he was asked to describe his life exactly as it was — without blame, without justification, without minimizing. Dwelling in reality meant seeing that his marriage was over and that his career had stagnated because of his own choices. It was devastating. It was also the most clarifying moment of his adult life. Three months later he had filed for divorce and enrolled in night school. Reality, however painful, is always more useful than comfortable illusion.

Dr. Glover had been raised to believe that expressing feelings was weak and manipulative. In his 30s, during a therapeutic experience, he was asked to identify what he was feeling. He went blank. After several minutes he said, 'I think I feel... nothing?' The facilitator smiled. 'Nothing is a feeling. What is nothing like?' That opened a door. Nice Guys who learn to express feelings — to actually name them and communicate them to safe people — experience a rapid and often dramatic reduction in the anxiety and resentment that has been driving their behavior.

content.label: Guidelines for Expressing Feelings
content.text: Use 'I' statements — 'I feel frustrated' rather than 'you make me frustrated.' Name the specific emotion rather than describing a behavior ('I feel lonely' rather than 'I feel like you don't care'). Share feelings with people who have earned the right to hear them — not everyone, not immediately, not to manipulate. Express feelings because it serves your own integrity and health, not to change someone else's behavior. The goal is not to perform feelings but to be honestly present.

Nolan came to therapy convinced he could not handle being alone, could not survive financial hardship, could not withstand his wife's anger. Dr. Glover gave him a mantra: 'I can handle it.' Over the next six months Nolan faced every fear he had named — his wife left him, he lost half his income, he had to sell the house. Each time he reached a moment he had been sure would destroy him, he found that he could, in fact, handle it. The mantra became not a platitude but a proven fact. Facing fear is the most direct path to power because it reveals the truth: you are stronger than you think.

Jake had been lying to his girlfriend Kisha for three years — small lies, about where he had been, what he had spent money on, what he was thinking — to avoid conflict. When he finally told her the truth about a business deal he had hidden from her, she was not as angry as he had feared. In fact, she said she felt closer to him than she had in months. Joel discovered the same thing: living with integrity, even when it required uncomfortable honesty, did not destroy his relationships. It rebuilt them on a foundation that could actually hold weight.

When a Nice Guy consistently lets others violate his boundaries, he teaches them that this is acceptable. He also teaches himself that he is not worth protecting. Setting a boundary is not an act of aggression — it is an act of self-respect that simultaneously tells the truth about what you will and will not accept. People who truly care about you will respect a boundary that is clearly and directly stated.

Identify one area of your life where you are trying to control an outcome or manage someone's perception of you. This week, practice surrendering that control — consciously choosing to let go of managing the result and trusting yourself to handle whatever happens. Then identify one boundary you have been failing to set. State it clearly, once, without over-explaining or apologizing, to the person who needs to hear it.

chapters.title: Reclaim Your Masculinity
chapters.keyIdea: Nice Guys are disconnected from men, from their own masculinity, and bonded to their mothers — reclaiming masculinity means connecting with men, getting strong, finding male role models, and seeing their fathers clearly.

content.text: In the post–World War II era, social forces combined to produce a generation of men who were disconnected from healthy male identity. Absent or traumatized fathers, feminist cultural messaging, and enmeshed relationships with mothers left many boys without a clear experience of what it meant to be a man. Nice Guys are the product of this environment: sensitive, empathic, domesticated — and deeply disconnected from their own masculine energy.

content.label: 4 Ways Social Conditioning Affected Nice Guys
content.text: (1) They were raised primarily by women and received an essentially feminine model of how to behave and what was important. (2) They were often given the implicit message that masculinity — or at least some core masculine traits — was problematic or dangerous. (3) They bonded with their mothers in ways that made pleasing women the central preoccupation of their lives. (4) They were cut off from healthy male role models who could have shown them what integrated masculinity looks like.

content.label: What Masculinity Actually Is
content.text: Masculinity is not domination, violence, or emotional numbness. It is a specific kind of energy — directional, action-oriented, boundaried, self-possessed. A masculine man is comfortable in his own skin. He knows what he wants and pursues it. He protects what matters to him. He does not shrink from challenge or conflict. He takes responsibility for his life without excusing or blaming. He is capable of both strength and tenderness. Most Nice Guys have never seen this modeled and don't know it's available to them.

Many Nice Guys are what Dr. Glover calls 'monogamous to their mothers' — psychologically fused with their mother in ways that have never fully separated. A boy who was enmeshed with his mother learned to read her emotional needs and make them his priority. As an adult, he carries that pattern into every relationship with women, unconsciously attempting to win the approval he never fully secured from his mother. Anita told Dr. Glover that her husband Gary was the most thoughtful, considerate man she had ever met — and that she couldn't stand it. She felt smothered. She had stopped being physically attracted to him. 'He's more like a girlfriend than a husband,' she said. Gary wasn't being a bad man. He was being his mother's son — in the wrong relationship.

Women do not want a man who is more like them. They want a man who is different from them — who brings a distinct, grounded, masculine presence to the relationship. When women say they want a sensitive man, they mean a man who is emotionally available, not a man who is emotionally dependent on their approval. The Nice Guy confuses the two. His sensitivity comes not from strength but from the constant anxious monitoring of whether the woman in front of him is pleased with him.

content.label: 4 Tools for Reclaiming Masculinity
content.text: (1) Connect with men — join a men's group, develop genuine male friendships, spend regular time with other men. Male energy is contagious and essential. (2) Get strong — develop your body through exercise, physical challenge, or competition. Physical strength builds psychological confidence. (3) Find male role models — identify two or three men whose integrated masculinity you admire and spend time in their presence. Ask them questions. Let them mentor you. (4) Examine your relationship with your father — see him clearly, neither idealizing nor demonizing him. Grieve what you didn't get. Appreciate what you did.

Alan was skeptical when Dr. Glover suggested he join a men's group. He had lots of female friends but few male ones — men seemed competitive and threatening to him. After three months in the group, he reported something unexpected: he was arguing with his wife less. What had changed? When he started getting genuine connection and validation from other men, he stopped needing his wife to be his entire emotional support system. She noticed the change immediately and told him he seemed more like himself. She initiated sex for the first time in months.

Jamie told her boyfriend's men's group that she had been dating a succession of 'bad boys' before meeting him. Bad boys, she explained, were exciting and attractive even though they treated her poorly. What she had found with her current boyfriend — a recovering Nice Guy — was a man who was both kind and strong. 'He's not a pushover,' she said. 'He pushes back. He has his own life. He's not always trying to please me. And I can't keep my hands off him.' The integration of masculine strength and genuine warmth is the combination women actually respond to.

Travis was 40 pounds overweight, unemployed, and struggling with alcohol when he came to therapy. His father had been a violent alcoholic. Travis had decided as a boy that being masculine meant being dangerous — and had spent his adult life being as un-masculine as possible. Dr. Glover suggested he join a gym. Travis resisted for months. When he finally started lifting weights, something shifted. The physical discipline required him to push through discomfort, to build something through consistent effort. Within a year he was sober, working, and had lost the weight. 'Getting strong wasn't just physical,' he said. 'It changed how I see myself.'

Matthew had idealized his absent father his entire life — convinced that if his father had been present, everything would have been different. In therapy he was asked to see his father clearly: a man who had made promises he didn't keep, who had chosen alcohol and other women over his family, who had never once asked Matthew how he was doing. Matthew spent a full session grieving this father — not the idealized fantasy but the real man. At the end he said, 'I've been waiting for him to show up my whole life. I think I can stop waiting now.' Seeing his father clearly freed him to stop organizing his life around a wound that was never going to be healed by the man who caused it.

In Robert Bly's Iron John, the old myth describes a ritual in which a boy is led away from his mother's world and initiated into the world of men by the masculine — by the Wild Man. Nice Guys missed this initiation. They can create it now, in their 30s and 40s and 50s, through conscious connection with men who carry integrated masculine energy. What you didn't get in childhood, you can still get — but you have to go find it.

Identify one man in your life whose masculinity you respect — not because he is dominant or aggressive, but because he seems comfortable in his own skin, takes responsibility for his life, and is both strong and genuinely kind. Spend time with him. Ask him how he became the man he is. Then identify one physical practice — lifting weights, martial arts, hiking, a team sport — and commit to it for 90 days. Notice what changes in how you feel about yourself.

chapters.title: Get The Love You Want: Success Strategies For Intimate Relationships
chapters.keyIdea: Nice Guys co-create the very dysfunctional relationships they complain about — getting the love they want requires approving of themselves, setting boundaries, seeing their partner as a mirror, and choosing differently.

content.text: Karl came to therapy convinced he was the victim of his girlfriend's dysfunction. She was moody, critical, and unpredictable. He had tried everything — being more patient, more understanding, more helpful — but nothing worked. As therapy progressed, a different picture emerged: Karl was not the passive victim of his girlfriend's chaos. He was an active participant who had chosen her for reasons rooted in his own childhood, and whose behaviors — caretaking, avoiding conflict, trying to fix her — were actively fueling the dysfunction he hated.

Toxic shame is the mortar that binds Nice Guys to dysfunctional relationships. Because Nice Guys believe they are fundamentally flawed, they are unconsciously drawn to partners who confirm this belief — partners who are critical, emotionally unavailable, or in need of constant rescue. Trying to win the love of this partner replays the original childhood wound and holds out the unconscious promise: if I can finally get this person to love me, it will prove I am not defective.

content.label: 6 Reasons Nice Guys Have Unsatisfying Relationships
content.text: (1) They seek approval rather than intimacy — performing for their partner rather than being present with them. (2) They hide their true selves — presenting only what they believe is acceptable, which makes genuine intimacy impossible. (3) They make their partner responsible for their emotional wellbeing — an impossible burden. (4) They create covert contracts — giving with expectations of return that are never stated. (5) They are bad enders — staying in wrong relationships long after they should leave. (6) They choose unavailable or dysfunctional partners — unconsciously re-creating familiar childhood dynamics.

content.label: Enmeshers and Avoiders: Two Nice Guy Patterns
content.text: Enmeshers are Nice Guys who try to get their needs met by bonding deeply and eliminating separateness. They are smothering, jealous, and threatened by their partner having an independent life. Avoiders are Nice Guys who get their needs met by maintaining distance — they are attracted to unavailable partners, create barriers to intimacy, and flee when relationships become too close. Both patterns are driven by the same fear: that if someone truly knows them, they will be found defective and abandoned.

Many Nice Guys unconsciously re-create their relationship with their mothers in their intimate partnerships. They are drawn to women who are emotionally similar to their mothers — critical, needy, withholding, or depressed — not because they enjoy the pain, but because the dynamic feels familiar and holds the unconscious promise of finally getting the approval they originally sought. Dr. Glover recognized this in himself: he had married a woman who was emotionally similar to his own unavailable mother, and he had spent years trying to use the same strategies that had failed in childhood — being good, being helpful, hiding his needs — and getting the same result.

content.label: Warning: The Path Forward Forks Here
content.text: When Nice Guys begin recovery, their intimate relationships will be significantly affected. Some relationships improve dramatically as the Nice Guy becomes more honest, direct, and boundaried. Others will not survive — they were built on the Nice Guy's dysfunction, and a healthier man is no longer compatible with them. This is not failure. A relationship ending because both people have grown is a success. The warning: do not use recovery as a weapon to punish your partner, and do not stop growing because your partner is threatened by your growth.

George had been performing for his wife for 11 years — always trying to anticipate her needs, always suppressing his own, always managing her moods. In therapy he began focusing exclusively on his own wants, feelings, and needs. He stopped trying to please her and started being honest about what he actually wanted. His wife was initially disoriented and then angry. Then, three months in, she told him she was feeling more attracted to him than she had in years. 'I finally feel like there's a real person here,' she said. 'I don't have to wonder what you actually think anymore.'

Setting limits is the foundation of a healthy relationship. Dr. Glover offers the Second Date Rule: never ask a woman on a second date if the first date wasn't completely enjoyable. And the Healthy Male Rule: never spend time with a woman who doesn't treat you well. These are not punishments — they are the expression of a fundamental belief that you deserve to be treated well and will not accept less. This stance, paradoxically, is one of the most attractive things a man can demonstrate.

Your partner is a perfect mirror. The things that bother you most about them are almost always pointing directly back at something in yourself — a need you are not taking responsibility for, a pattern you are recreating, a wound you have not addressed. This is not blame; it is information. The relationship that frustrates you most is also, potentially, your most powerful vehicle for growth — if you can stop seeing yourself as the victim and start asking what your presence in this dynamic is telling you about yourself.

After months of work, Karl stopped trying to fix his girlfriend Danita's chaos and started asking what his attraction to it said about him. He recognized that her unpredictability felt familiar — his mother had been the same way, and he had spent his childhood trying to anticipate her moods and keep things calm. Danita wasn't a problem to be solved. She was a gift — a precise mirror showing him exactly where his unfinished childhood business lived. When he stopped trying to fix her and started working on himself, the relationship transformed. Not because Danita changed — but because Karl did.

Joe had a dog that barked and snapped at guests. He spent years correcting, apologizing for, and managing the dog's behavior. A trainer told him: 'Every time you apologize for the dog, you reinforce the behavior. The dog is doing exactly what you've trained it to do — getting your full attention every time it acts out.' Joe began applying the same logic to his relationship: every time he caretook, apologized, or rushed in to smooth things over, he was training his partner that dysfunction got a response. When he stopped reinforcing the behavior, the dynamic shifted.

content.label: What to Look for in a New Partner
content.text: A person who is available — emotionally and practically. A person who is kind — to you, to others, to strangers. A person who takes responsibility for their own life and does not consistently blame others. A person who has their own interests, friendships, and sense of self separate from you. A person who can handle direct, honest communication. A person who treats you well not because you performed for them but simply because they want to. Most Nice Guys have never been in a relationship with someone who meets these criteria — and the normalcy of it initially feels boring. Do not confuse familiar chaos with passion.

Look honestly at your current or most recent significant relationship. Ask: What childhood dynamic does this relationship mirror? What covert contracts have I been operating with? What am I reinforcing by how I respond to my partner's behavior? Then identify one concrete change — not in your partner's behavior, but in your own — that would shift the dynamic. Make that change, and observe what happens over the next month.

chapters.title: Get The Sex You Want: Success Strategies For Satisfying Sex
chapters.keyIdea: Nice Guys sabotage their own sex lives through shame, avoidance, compulsion, and performance — getting the sex they want requires coming out of the closet about sexual shame, taking responsibility for their own pleasure, and refusing to settle for bad sex.

content.text: Take everything written about Nice Guys in this book — their shame, their sacrifice of self, their approval seeking, their doing the opposite of what works, their covert contracts, their dishonesty, their loss of masculine energy — put it all in a container, shake it up, open the lid, and you have a pretty good view of how Nice Guys do sex. For Nice Guys, sex is where all of their abandonment experiences, toxic shame, and dysfunctional survival mechanisms are focused and magnified. Every Nice Guy with whom Dr. Glover has ever worked has had some significant problem with sex.

content.label: 5 Common Sexual Problems of Nice Guys
content.text: (1) Not getting enough — the most common complaint, blame often directed at a 'sexually inhibited' partner. (2) Settling for less than satisfying sex — believing bad sex is better than no sex. (3) Sexual dysfunction — inability to get or maintain an erection, premature ejaculation. (4) Sexual repression — claiming little interest in sex while secretly engaged in hidden sexual activity. (5) Compulsive sexual behavior — masturbation, pornography addiction, affairs, peep shows, 900 numbers, cybersex, prostitution. It is Nice Guys themselves who are masters at making sure their sex lives are less than satisfying.

All Nice Guys have shame and fear about being sexual and about being sexual beings. This is the most difficult concept for Nice Guys to understand and accept about themselves. Every time a Nice Guy is in a sexual situation, he must negotiate through a lifetime of unconscious baggage: memories of childhood experiences that made him feel bad; the pain of unmet needs; sexually wounded parents; religious guilt; covert sexual bonds to his mother; trauma of sexual violations; early experiences wrapped in secrecy; distorted images from pornography; shame of hidden behaviors; and memories of previous rejections.

Alan was a vagiphobia poster child — Dr. Glover coined the term for the syndrome where the penis tries to stay out of vaginas. Alan entered sexualized relationships outside his marriage but never consummated any of them. 'Flirting without fucking.' On a business trip, he and an attractive coworker ended up in a hot tub where she sat on his lap and they kissed passionately. Even though he was aroused, he turned down her invitation to her room so as not to 'jeopardize their working relationship.' He never directly initiated sex with his wife because he believed women thought sex was bad — and that if he was too direct, they would think he was bad.

Trying to be a good lover is a surprisingly effective mechanism for avoiding real intimacy. Terrance came to therapy for premature ejaculation. He would orally stimulate his girlfriend until she had two or three orgasms before penetration, then attempt another orgasm vaginally — so focused on her pleasure that he told her he didn't care if he never had an orgasm as long as she was satisfied. She was considering postponing the wedding. When Nice Guys set out to be great lovers, they create boring, routine, do-what-worked-last-time sex. Trying to be a great lover insures that a Nice Guy will not have many passionate, reciprocal, spontaneous, or intimate sexual experiences.

Dr. Glover's theory: the nicer the guy, the darker the sexual secrets. Lyle was a devout Christian, Sunday school teacher, good husband — apparently perfect. He had been secretly addicted to pornography since age nine. By mid-marriage his compulsive behavior had expanded to adult videos, peep shows, strip bars, 900 numbers, and cybersex chat rooms. He almost never had actual sex with his wife. When his wife found a phone bill and called the numbers, everything crashed. After individual therapy, Lyle joined a 12-step group for sexual addicts. Every time he revealed a secret thought or act, he felt a weight lift from his shoulders. As he revealed his fear and shame to safe people, he found himself less interested in his hidden, compulsive behaviors. He and his wife began to enjoy a physical closeness he had once tried to avoid.

Trying to be 'nice' to attract women — the strategy formed in adolescence — robs a man of his life energy. The more a Nice Guy seeks approval and tries to 'do it right,' the tighter he clamps a lid down on any energy that might actually attract a person to him. Women consistently report that even though they may be initially drawn to a Nice Guy's pleasing demeanor, over time they find it difficult to get excited about having sex with him. There is very little about the Nice Guy persona to flip a switch or arouse a prospective partner.

A Nice Guy's wife put it precisely: 'I could tell him it would really turn me on if he set himself on fire. He would gladly do it and think he was getting good sex because it made me happy.' Aaron and Hannah's bedroom illustrates settling for bad sex: Aaron approaches Hannah indirectly, massaging her slowly to avoid alarming her, completely tuning out awareness of his own body and focusing entirely on her arousal. He fantasizes about his secretary to climax. When he finally finishes, he immediately checks on Hannah's emotional state. He rolls over feeling empty and resentful.

content.label: 4 Strategies for Getting Good Sex
content.text: (1) Coming out of the closet — bringing sexual shame and fear into the open where they can be released. This step cannot be skipped. (2) Taking matters into their own hands — practicing healthy masturbation: no goal, no pornography, no fantasy, just paying attention to what feels good and taking responsibility for one's own pleasure. (3) Saying 'no' to bad sex — you have to let go of what you've got to get what you want. (4) Following the example of the bull moose — being fierce, self-confident, and sexually proud, because the greatest aphrodisiac is self-confidence.

Healthy masturbation is not compulsive masturbation. It is a process of letting sexual energy unfold with no goal or destination — not about orgasms, not requiring pornography or fantasy, not using trance or dissociation to stay distracted from shame and fear. It is about learning to pay attention to what feels good. Most of all, it is about accepting sole responsibility for one's sexual pleasure. The logic: until a Nice Guy can be sexual with himself without shame, he won't be able to be sexual with another person without shame. Until he can give pleasure to himself, he won't be able to receive pleasure from someone else. Until he can be sexually present with himself, he won't be able to be present with a partner. Terrance worked on healthy masturbation; when he told his fiancée, she expressed relief — she had been faking it because she felt burdened by his expectation of multiple orgasms. They married and discarded their old way of doing sex for a more intimate, connected experience.

content.label: Notes on Pornography and Fantasy
content.text: Pornography creates unrealistic expectations, addicts men to bodies and body parts, becomes a substitute for real relationships, creates a trance that allows men to be sexual while staying distracted from shame and fear, and compounds shame because it is usually kept hidden. If a Nice Guy is going to use pornography, do it openly — that alone tends to break the trance and take the buzz out of it. Fantasy is a form of dissociation — purposefully leaving one's body. While some sex therapists advocate fantasy as a way of improving a sex life, it is actually the best way to kill it. Fantasizing during sex is like thinking about a Big Mac while eating a gourmet meal. Fantasy only distracts from shame and fear — or covers up the fact that a person is having bad sex.

Good sex is two people taking full responsibility for meeting their own needs. It has no goal, is free of agendas and expectations. It is an unfolding of sexual energy — two people revealing themselves in the most intimate and vulnerable of ways, each focusing on their own pleasure, passion, and arousal while staying connected to those same things in their partner. Aaron's sexual moratorium lasted six months. He focused on his own needs, expressed his feelings to Hannah directly, told her when he was angry, told her when he didn't want to listen to her work problems. Hannah began making sexual advances toward him. She told him she felt freer to move toward him since he wasn't pursuing her. After six months, when they resumed sex, he felt much more connected to his wife.

Find a safe place and talk about your sexual history — your earliest sexual memory, childhood experiences, any sexual violation or trauma, your first sexual experience. Also discuss the ways you have acted out sexually, and the aspects of your sexuality you have the hardest time looking at. Then set aside time to practice healthy masturbation — no pornography, no fantasy, no goal — just paying attention to what feels good in your body. As recovering Nice Guys release their sexual shame and fear and refuse to settle for bad sex, they put themselves in the position to embrace sex as the cosmic force it truly is.

chapters.title: Get The Life You Want: Discover Your Passion And Purpose In Life, Work, And Career
chapters.keyIdea: Nice Guys are intelligent men living at a fraction of their potential — held back by fear, perfectionism, self-sabotage, and deprivation thinking — and getting the life they want requires facing fears, asking for help, letting go of perfection, and making their own rules.

content.text: If there were no limits on your life — where would you live? What would you be doing? What kind of work would you be engaged in? As you look at the reality of your life, ask yourself two questions: are you creating the life you want? And if not, why not? The Nice Guys Dr. Glover has worked with have been intelligent, industrious, and competent individuals. While most are at least moderately successful, the majority have not lived up to their full abilities or potential. This is perhaps the greatest tragedy wrought by the Nice Guy Syndrome — countless intelligent and talented men wasting their lives and wallowing in the mire of mediocrity.

content.label: 7 Reasons Nice Guys Fall Short of Their Potential
content.text: (1) Fear — of failure, mistakes, and paradoxically, of success itself. (2) Trying to do it right — perfectionism that kills creative energy. (3) Trying to do everything themselves — inability to ask for help or delegate. (4) Self-sabotage — wasting time, procrastinating, not finishing projects. (5) A distorted self-image — a deep-seated sense of inadequacy and an emotional and cognitive glass ceiling. (6) Deprivation thinking — a scarcity paradigm that makes them manipulative, controlling, and resentful. (7) Staying stuck in dysfunctional but familiar systems — re-creating the dysfunction of their childhood in their jobs.

If there is one common factor at the core of every problem experienced by Nice Guys, it would be fear. Fear prevents a Nice Guy from demanding the raise he has been promised, going back to school, quitting a job he despises, starting the business of his dreams, or living where he really wants to live. Right alongside the fears of failure and disaster is the paradoxical fear of success. Nice Guys are typically afraid that if they truly succeed, they will be found out as frauds, criticized, unable to handle the increased expectations, and that they will do something to mess everything up. Rather than facing these fears, Nice Guys settle for operating at a fraction of their full potential.

Trying to 'do it right' and following the 'rules' is the most effective way to put a lid on creative energy. It kills passion and prevents Nice Guys from living up to their full potential: striving for perfection keeps them focused on imperfections; seeking external validation keeps them stuck in mediocrity; attempting to hide flaws prevents them from taking risks or trying something new; following the rules makes them rigid, cautious, and fearful. Because of these self-imposed limits, many Nice Guys are dissatisfied, bored, or unhappy with their life and vocation.

As children, Nice Guys grew up believing it was bad or dangerous for them to have needs — convinced that if they were going to have anything in life, it would be up to them. Consequently, Nice Guys are terrible receivers. They are terrified of asking for help. They are completely miserable when others try to give to them. They have difficulty delegating to others. Because they believe they have to do it all themselves, Nice Guys are jacks-of-all-trades but masters of none. This childhood conditioning ensures that they will never be all they can be in any area of life.

Because of their fear of success, Nice Guys are masters of self-sabotage. Being truly great invites too much unwanted attention and scrutiny — the bright lights of success threaten to illuminate their self-perceived cracks and flaws. So Nice Guys find many creative ways to make sure they are never too successful: if they don't start something, they won't fail; if they don't finish something, they won't be criticized; if they have too much going on at once, they won't have to do any one thing well; if they have enough good excuses, people won't expect too much of them.

At their core, all Nice Guys believe they are not important or good enough — the root of their toxic shame. As a result of their inability to fix, please, or take care of one or more parents, many Nice Guys developed a deep-seated sense of inadequacy. The result is an emotional and cognitive glass ceiling. This invisible lid prevents Nice Guys from being all they can be. If they do try to rise above it, they bump their heads and tumble down to more familiar territory. Russell illustrates deprivation thinking: a successful salesman with a six-figure income and $30,000 in his checking account — yet he would not allow his wife to buy a $9.00 video for their children at Costco if it wasn't in the budget. His miserly father had singled him out for critical treatment and cut him out of his will. Russell viewed the entire world through lenses clouded by deprivation.

Charlie could have been the poster child for passionless, underachieving Nice Guys. He was stuck in a job he hated, still working the same position he'd held before college despite earning his engineering degree, and his employer had broken a promise of promotion. His single passion was flying — he'd begun taking lessons but could never seem to complete the requirements to get his pilot's license. When he found Dr. Glover's website and read the description of a Nice Guy, he was mortified. It took him six months to send a first email, two more months for a second. Then he made a decision: if something frightened him that much, he would face the fear and do it. Over the next eighteen months Charlie crawled until he could walk, took baby steps until he could run. He got his pilot's license, confronted his father, changed flying schools, left his old job, and landed a position at an engineering firm that believed he was capable and talented.

content.label: Charlie's 6-Step Formula for Getting the Life You Want
content.text: (1) Very first, before anything else could happen, I had to stop being a victim. (2) I began by setting boundaries. At first they were small ones and they grew with time. (3) From the boundaries being set and respected, I started believing in myself. (4) Honesty came along somewhere during this time. (5) Believing that I am an adult, I have an education, and I am qualified to take on the role of an industrial engineer. (6) I always knew that my previous employer was dysfunctional, and that it was comfortable for a reason. When I finally realized and accepted that I did not need that system to survive, I could finally move on.

Dr. Glover's book took six years to complete — not because of bad luck, but because of perfectionism. What started as a few chapters written spontaneously for a handful of clients became paralyzed the moment people began suggesting best-seller lists and Oprah appearances. The original manuscript grew to four times its present length as he tried to include everything he knew. His wife half-seriously threatened to leave him if he didn't finish. The breakthrough came when a wise person suggested he give himself permission to never publish the book. He felt an immediate sense of relief. Once he let go of the burden of perfection and returned to his original goal — helping a few men live better lives — the book came together. Trying to do it right had sucked the life out of the project. Letting go and letting it just be 'good enough' set him free to embrace his passion and create something of lasting value.

Phil wanted to be rich. He was good-looking, intelligent, outgoing, and funny — but always fell short due to shortcuts, procrastination, and insecurities about whether he deserved what he wanted. His greatest roadblock was his difficulty asking people for help. In his men's group, he lamented the lack of sex in his marriage. Dr. Glover asked if he had asked his wife to have sex with him. He said no. The next week, Phil was grinning: 'My wife and I had sex.' 'How?' the group asked. 'I asked,' was Phil's simple reply. His wife said she was fine about it — she just hadn't thought he was interested since he hadn't asked in a long time. Then Phil asked the group to help him replace the single-pane windows on his house. Like pulling teeth — but every man said yes. About a month later they all came over and had the equivalent of an old-fashioned barn raising. As long as Phil tried to do everything himself, he struggled to get what he wanted. Once he started asking for help and letting people be there for him, his life began to turn around.

Sal ran a body shop for a cheap, shortsighted uncle with limited resources and dissatisfied employees — and actually operated on the assumption that a profitable business could be created from this. Every week he arrived at group and popped Tylenol for the stress headache it caused. When the group asked about his passion, he paused before answering: 'I've always wanted to teach martial arts. There is no way it could ever happen though.' With each option the group proposed, Sal grew noticeably tenser — his eyes reflected a terror as if being interrogated with cattle prods. In most situations, Nice Guys aren't victims to others; they victimize themselves. Making significant life changes doesn't require chucking everything. A mortgage, a wife, children — these are excuses. It means seeing them for what they are and taking small steps in the direction one wants to be going.

content.label: The No More Mr. Nice Guy Rules for Getting the Life You Want
content.text: If it frightens you, do it. Don't settle — every time you settle, you get exactly what you settled for. Put yourself first. No matter what happens, you will handle it. Whatever you do, do it 100%. If you do what you've always done, you will get what you've always got. You are the only person on this planet responsible for your needs, wants, and happiness. Ask for what you want. Be clear and direct. Learn to say 'no.' Don't make excuses. Let people help you. Be honest with yourself. Do not let anyone treat you badly. No one. Ever. Remove yourself from a bad situation instead of waiting for it to change. Stop blaming — victims never succeed. Live with integrity. Be good to yourself. Think abundance. Face difficult situations and conflict head on. Don't do anything in secret. Do it now. Give yourself room to fail — there are no mistakes, only learning experiences. Control is an illusion. Let go. Let life happen.

Write down three things you want to make happen in your life. Then write a personal affirmation that will take you where you want to go and post it where you can see it every day. Share your dreams and your affirmation with a safe person. Then identify one thing that frightens you — a raise, a business, going back to school, pursuing a lifelong goal — write down exactly how you will confront that fear, and take one small step toward it today. Remember: the only thing stopping you from having the kind of life you really want is you.

chapters.title: Epilogue
chapters.keyIdea: The tools and insights in No More Mr. Nice Guy work — as you learn to approve of yourself, you discover an unimaginable ability to love and accept love, and the unlimited freedom to be exactly who you are.

content.text: It took six years to write this book. During this time Dr. Glover worked with countless Nice Guys and their partners, averaging three No More Mr. Nice Guy men's groups per week — over 1,800 hours of group time alone. He has watched countless men go from being helpless, passive, controlling, and resentful victims to becoming empowered, integrated males. He has seen numerous relationships dramatically improve, and just as many die an overdue death. He has received responses from men and women all over the world who have seen themselves or someone they love in the description of the Nice Guy. Based on all of these observations, here is his greatest discovery: the tools and insights presented in this book work.

Having finished reading this book, start again from the beginning. Take the time to do the Breaking Free exercises. Find a safe person or group to assist you on your journey of recovery from the Nice Guy Syndrome. If you are in a relationship, ask your partner to read the book and share the insights you are discovering about yourself. Working the program of recovery presented in No More Mr. Nice Guy is one of the greatest gifts you can give yourself and your loved ones.

As you learn to approve of yourself, you will discover within you an unimaginable ability to love and accept love and to live life to its fullest. This kind of expansiveness is initially frightening, but it is the essence of who you are and what you are meant to be. With this discovery of your true self comes unlimited freedom — freedom to be just who you are, freedom to stop seeking approval, freedom to start getting what you want.
